hints.vocative: Vocative looks the same for 1st decl., but no one is addressing her — she is the subject
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'regina' in form but context shows it is the nominative subject
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'reginae' — 'regina' is nominative here

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'reginae' ends in -ae (1st declension)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'reginae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'reginae' ends in -ae

hints.dative: While 'gravi' could be dative, it modifies 'cura' (ablative) — so it is ablative
hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'gravis' — 'gravi' is ablative singular
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'gravis' — 'gravi' with -i ending is ablative (3rd decl. adj.)

steps.prompt: What declension is 'gravis, gravis' (as a 3rd decl. adjective)?

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'gravis' ends in -is (3rd declension)
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'gravis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'gravis' ends in -is

steps.reveal: saucia = wounded (nom. fem. sg., predicate describing 'regina')

hints.ablative: Ablative feminine would be 'saucia' too, but here it agrees with nominative 'regina'
hints.accusative: Accusative plural neuter would be 'saucia', but here it is nom. fem. sg. with 'regina'
hints.vocative: Vocative looks the same, but no one addresses the queen — it is nominative predicate

steps.reveal: cura = by/with passion (abl. sg., ablative of cause)

hints.nominative: While 'cura' looks nominative, here it is ablative of cause — wounded 'by' passion
hints.vocative: Vocative would look the same, but 'cura' is ablative of cause with 'saucia'
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'curae' — 'cura' here is ablative

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'curae' ends in -ae (1st declension)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'curae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'curae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: What is the function of 'At' in this sentence?
steps.reveal: At = But (marks a shift of scene to Dido)

hints.preposition: 'At' is not a preposition — prepositions govern nouns. 'At' is a conjunction meaning 'but'
hints.interjection: 'At' is not an interjection — it is an adversative conjunction introducing contrast
hints.temporal conjunction: Temporal conjunctions like 'cum' mark time — 'at' marks contrast/opposition

translation: But the queen, already wounded by grievous passion
